title: Chocolate chip cookie bars
subtitle: American cookie dough pressed into a tin and baked
excerpt: Cookie bars are chocolate chip cookie dough pressed into a rectangular tin and baked as a single slab, then cut into squares or bars once cooled. They have the same flavour as a chocolate chip cookie but the centre stays softer and chewier because the dough is thicker. Faster than making individual cookies and better for feeding a crowd.
sourceNotes: Bar cookies are a firmly established category of American domestic baking, traced in recipe collections from the mid-twentieth century as a practical adaptation of drop-cookie recipes for serving to larger groups. Ruth Wakefield's Toll House cookie recipe of the 1930s is the foundation for most American chocolate chip cookie formulas. Pressing the dough into a tin rather than shaping individual cookies is the home baker's shortcut for parties and bake sales.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cut bars in a single layer, then store in a bag for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake a day ahead and store covered at room temperature. They are better the next day once set.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 20 to 24 minutes; the top should be set and pale golden but the centre still a little soft; it firms as it cools

content.text: The dough is identical to a standard chocolate chip cookie dough. The difference is that you press it evenly into a lined tin rather than portioning it out. The tin thickness means the centre takes longer to set than the outside: the bars are done when the top is set and pale gold, but the very centre still feels slightly underdone when pressed. It will firm to a dense, chewy centre as it cools. Do not cut until completely cool or the bars will crumble.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line a 30 x 20 cm rectangular tin with baking paper. Beat 175 g softened butter with 150 g light brown sugar and 75 g caster sugar until combined and slightly creamy. Beat in 2 large eggs and 2 tsp vanilla extract. Stir in 225 g plain flour, half a teaspoon each of bicarbonate of soda and salt. Fold in 200 g chocolate chips. Press the dough evenly into the tin, using wet fingers or the back of a spoon.

Bake for 20 to 24 minutes until the top is set and pale gold and the edges have pulled slightly from the tin. The centre should still feel just slightly soft when pressed: it will firm as it cools. Leave in the tin on a rack for at least 30 minutes before cutting. Lift out using the baking paper and cut into bars or squares.

items.problem: The bars are cakey and crumbly rather than chewy
items.solution: The eggs were too beaten, incorporating too much air, or the bake time was too long. Mix the dough until just combined after adding the flour, and remove from the oven while the centre is still soft.

items.problem: The bars stuck to the tin
items.solution: Leave enough baking paper overhanging the sides to use as handles for lifting. Even a well-greased tin may not release cleanly; the paper overhang is the reliable method.
